page: Microsoft Azure E64a_v4 is a memory-optimized virtual machine instance belonging to the EAv4 family. It is powered by an AMD EPYC 7452 processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture, featuring 64 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 32 physical cores with 2 threads per core. The processor operates at a clock speed of 3.35 GHz. The instance is equipped with 512.0 GB of RAM, providing a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Additionally, it includes 1638 GB of local storage and runs on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor.

Performance benchmarks indicate a strong multi-threaded capability alongside moderate single-core throughput. The instance achieved a strong rating in the passmark CPU score of 52454.92 and the passmark memory score of 2843.71, alongside a high multi-threaded bzip3 decompression speed of 260.47 GB/sec. However, single-core performance is a notable tradeoff, with stress-ng single-core CPU performance rating in the bottom 10% at 776.78. Memory bandwidth is stable, registering 106.39 GB/sec for large block read-only operations and 52.53 GB/sec for large block write operations.

Given its high memory density and local storage capacity, the E64a_v4 is highly suited for memory-intensive workloads such as relational databases, in-memory caching systems, and large-scale data processing. The qualitative cost efficiency of this instance is favorable for enterprise applications requiring substantial memory footprints without the added cost of dedicated GPU hardware. It is less suited for single-threaded tasks or heavy LLM inference, as evidenced by weak prompt processing speeds on larger models.
description: Microsoft Azure E64a_v4 is an x86_64 memory-optimized virtual machine from the EAv4 family, powered by an AMD EPYC 7452 processor running at 3.35 GHz. It features 64 dedicated vCPUs, 512.0 GB of RAM, and 1638 GB of local storage. Operating on Microsoft Hyper-V, this instance offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Benchmark results show strong multi-threaded performance, highlighted by a passmark CPU score of 52454.92 and a passmark memory score of 2843.71. However, single-core performance represents a tradeoff, with stress-ng single-core results in the bottom 10%. This hardware profile is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-dense enterprise workloads, making it highly suitable for large databases, in-memory caching, and data processing applications that do not require GPU acceleration.
